Quick question. What does direct response even mean? You know, we've all heard the term maybe even thought we were using and applying it correctly, but where did it come from and what does it even mean? So here's a certain definition that you might pull if you Google it. Direct response marketing is a type of marketing that elicits a specific measured response resulting from a consumer's direct response to a marketer. Direct response marketing facilitates the delivery of a call to action and outcome via direct or online interaction for immediate feedback and response. Okay, so that's a bit of a mouthful, measured specific action fair enough, and it kind of all made sense for the time when direct response was born. It was originally coined by this gentleman named Josiah Wellwood. So he was this Englishman who is credited for sending out the first catalog and also being credited as a first person to actually use the term. And to put that into context, this is 1872, which is about 40 years after Canada was born and people were still using carrier pigeons or something like that as a means of communication. So while direct response has really remained relevant and effective and producing who knows how many billions of dollars in the 150 years since its inception, I'm willing to gamble and bet that the definition is just a little bit too short-sighted and one-dimensional for the tools we have available to us today, as well as the sophistication level and skepticism of our audiences. So in a world of pixels, cookies, and crazy smart email automation software and even crazier and smarter customers, I think it's time for a new definition. I really want to create a new definition, one that's contextually relevant. We're no longer just sending mailers to people without having any clue where they're at as a prospect. So can we redefine it to be more contextually relevant, to be adaptable, to actually be two-dimensional, have that conversation, one that adapts to the fears, hopes, and objections of your prospect? And can we actually rephrase it a little to create the context for a response instead of trying to force one? We now have the tools to adapt our conversation to actually create this context and I think it's really time we leverage that. So that's why I am willing to create this new definition. What would direct response 2.0 mean? And where this is coming from is this thesis that your customer's response will only be as strong as the questions that elicit it. Let me say that again. Your customer's response will only be as strong as the questions that elicit it. And that is why collaborative selling or one-on-one selling is so effective and gets 10 times the conversion rates of typical online marketing methods or direct response. Think about all the questions that a coach will ask or a salesperson will ask during that enrollment call. It's 90% asking questions, hearing the response from the prospect and adapting the sales message accordingly. Now of course that is one-on-one selling and of course we're not going to be able to fully replicate that environment. But the closer we can extract the elements of what makes that effective, mostly the questions that are going on and adapting our messages accordingly, the closer we could come to bridging that gap, that 10x gap in conversion rates between typical online marketing and one-on-one enrollment calls. So that's why as part of direct response 2.0 as we upgrade the way we perceive marketing and direct response as a whole, here's what I want to include within that definition. Direct response 2.0 is intuitive. We're no longer just going for the superficial two-dimensional data, but we're actually piercing levels deeper, asking what it means, what insights can we as a marketer, as an intuitive marketer gather from what is being presented to us instead of taking it for face value. It's empathy driven. This is a skill that we all have that don't tap into when it comes to market. You know, we try to press the persuasion triggers instead of just going a little more subcutaneous and trying to really tap into what that feeling is. It anticipates the questions customers will have across every step. One of the practices I do when I write copy is I pretend that I am in a one-on-one enrollment call with this prospect and I just kind of have that conversation in my head and see what are they asking in this moment and how can I preempt that? How can I preempt that and present the answers to the questions in their head before they've even asked that? You know, as a side benefit, that's what generates those, oh my god, it's like you read my mind moments. Another thing it does is it challenges the prospect's beliefs and self-imposed limitations and it makes a new reality feel exciting, believable, and safe. So those are the main components of direct response 2.0, but it begs the question, even with this new definition, can we really 10X conversions? I know it's a crazy claim to make and it's not a claim, it's a thesis. It's a thesis that there is this 10X gap between one-on-one selling and enrollment calls and digital marketing methods. So while we can't fully replace that one-on-one dynamic, the goal here really is to extract the major levers, the main ingredients of what makes that one-on-one dynamic work so well, you know, the things that make people feel heard, seen, and understood, and do it for 60,000 leads in a single afternoon. You know, and to me that is a pretty special time when we could do those things at scale. And one of the best feelings I get as a copywriter or a marketer is when my clients inboxes get inundated with replies from clients saying, it's like you read my mind. I think I had one person who claimed my third eye was overactive or something like that. So there's nothing mystical about it. It comes down to being willing to just sit with, sit with it long enough to really envision being with that customer, preempt those questions, and make your copy a response to what those questions would be based on those insights you gathered and really generated through your own insight. So that's really what I want to challenge you to do, you know, to ask questions that go beyond the data, beyond what a customer would even be able to reveal about themselves to themselves, let alone the person asking, you know, and the pinnacle of this method, what we're really trying to achieve at the end of the day is to really give customers the sense that we know them better than they know themselves. That we know what they're going to ask before they even ask it. You know, and that's why everything I write is question based, you know, and you're going to see how we apply this direct response 2.0 method in fuller detail in the next module. But everything I write initially starts with a question I've answered. You know, I do spend some time with the quantitative data, but then I ask my own questions, and I develop my own internal rendering of it. And that's what really leads to that magnetic copy that just really generates those, oh my god, this person gets me moments. And those are the moments that build trust and authority and credibility, and really play a major part in the conversion. So here's the underlying thing. If the response will only be as strong as the questions that elicit it, then we better start asking some better questions. And then the next module, you're going to get the 47 golden questions workbook. These are really the core questions that I ask in JournalOn while preparing planning and executing a funnel. You know, sometimes I will ask extra questions, you know, when I'm writing specific theme plates, and you're going to get those as well. But these are the questions that I will work on myself and with my clients, especially when I'm taking on a new project and have to get up to speed in a matter of days, you know. And the copywriters in this program will definitely empathize, you know, you are writing for multiple clients, multiple different audiences. It's kind of overwhelming. And unless you really have this clear and effective way to quickly onboard yourself to the emotions of that audience, it becomes pretty difficult. So this is something that I initially created for myself. These are the 47 golden questions I will fuel every phase of your funnel. And yeah, you won't have to answer them all at once in module two. They are going to be sprinkled out throughout the course, but you are going to get that workbook. And we are going to be pretty much making it the Bible for this course. Another side benefit to answering these questions, and I really urge you not to skip it to spend the time to do it because it really does make writing your copy faster. But the side benefit is you're also going to gain clarity on your own business and offerings. You know, by answering these questions, you're going to see if you're really hitting those sweet spots and maybe addressing some core hidden needs that your audience may be experiencing. I don't exaggerate when I say that answering this has led to new sales hooks. It's led to new ideas for offerings, new aspects of your business that address and counter some of those objections and deep seated beliefs that your audience would be feeling. So I really encourage you to spend some time with this. You know, many of the copywriters that I speak with regularly will also affirm that they spend more time in the research phase, more time in cultivating these insights and building these ideas than they do actually executing. And that's what I want to encourage every product creator, entrepreneur, coach, consultant, course creator listening to this to do, to give yourself the time to actually sit and simmer with these questions, to go down those rabbit holes and see what you uncover about your audience and how your offer connects to it. At the end of the day, it comes down to this. You know, it comes down to asking, why do 97% of perfectly targeted leads not buy? You know, most of us are just scrambling around how to get that 2.5% to 2.8% or that 2% to 3% or whatever it is. We're looking for those very small incremental bumps and that's okay. That will get us further. It will bring more revenue into your business. But at the end of the day, the big quantum leaps in business don't come from trying to optimize 2 to 3% or 3 to 4%. It comes from asking why do 97% of perfectly targeted leads not buy? And part of what we're doing here, you know, is really not just kind of asking these questions for our own benefit. But, you know, to get the best results, we're actually trying to build up our prospects ability to respond. You know, build up our prospects ability to respond. If 97% of people aren't buying, there is a disconnect. There's a disconnect in our prospects ability to raise their hand and say, yes, this was exactly what I needed presented exactly in the way I needed to hear it. And I am going to respond now with my purchase. So what we're doing in this method is we're also building up our prospects ability to respond. And we're doing that by empowering them where they've been disempowered. We're getting them present and focused with things they've neglected and avoided in the past. You know, we avoid things that are painful to us. You know, and we as marketers, yes, we try to agitate it, but maybe before we agitate it, we need to kind of create that space that distancing that oakeness to even venture into that place. So these are things we look at. We also help them release constrictions and fears around their capacity to meet it. You know, so it's not just about amplifying fear. Sometimes, you know, the best thing you could do is actually help reduce it, tone it down, you know, and enhance their capacity and ability to meet that fear and do something with it. You know, and we give them those new beliefs, understandings and inner shifts to move forward. So it's not a matter of just scaring your prospect into a sale. You know, you know, fear is a very strong emotion, but it's also one that keeps people from even looking at something. It leads to avoidance just as much as it leads to action. So we have to be very mindful about how we layer that in. And also the counterpunches we do to make it okay to address that fear. We don't want people scared. We want people empowered to meet the fear. So there is a difference in that. And that is something we are going to be working with a little later on. So at the end of the day, I just want to leave you with the idea that the secret to higher conversions isn't about more persuasion tricks, but getting more present with who we're talking to and what they need to hear from us in any given moment to move forward. So the workbook of questions you're going to get is literally how I get myself super steeped and present with my audience or at least the audiences of my clients within hours. You know, you're going to get that book. You're going to get the walkthrough of the questions and the next module. But for now, I'm going to take a little break. I'm going to meet you in the final lesson of module one soon, where you're actually going to get a few more little mindset shifts that you need to adopt and cultivate to be a successful conversion coach. I'll see you in the next lesson.
